answer:
The following works in the corpus employ the figure of the road or path (i.e., as a metaphor or rhetorical device symbolizing a journey, moral/ethical course, spiritual wayfaring, life's progression, or similar conceptual progression, rather than merely literal thoroughfares). This is drawn exhaustively from corpus-wide searches; many other mentions (e.g., in the Smṛti texts) are strictly literal/legal (e.g., public roads, crossroads for ordeals, travel abroad). Works are listed alphabetically by slug, with representative figurative examples cited.[^1][^2][^3][^4][^5][^6][^7][^8]

**allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c** (*Bang-E-Dara Part 1*): Paths as life's quest or spiritual journey (e.g., "on life's long road you roam"; "dust upon the road to Hejaz' holy light").[^9]

**allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-2-9bc58c** (*Bang-E-Dara Part 2*): Paths of destiny, motion, or trial (e.g., "a thorny path will rise"; "path of destiny"). 

**allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8** (*Bang-E-Dara Part 3*): Paths of union, sorrow, or cosmic travel (e.g., "roads of glory sleep"; "no firefly's spark to light the path").[^10]

**charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-i-6da8ce** (*Nouveau traité d'économie: VOL I*): Paths as routes to moral, industrial, or civilizational progress ("paths of industry and morality"; "true road to liberty").[^11]

**cynewulf-andreas-07b573** (*Andreas*): Sea-kennings as figurative paths/journeys of trial, exile, or divine mission (e.g., "flood-path"; "path of pain"; "border-paths").[^12]

**cynewulf-elene-d2d132** (*Elene*): Paths/ways of sea-voyage or moral turning ("swan-road"). 

**cynewulf-juliana-9a2157** (*Juliana*): Spiritual/moral divergence ("stark road").[^13]

**mirza-ghalib-diwan-e-ghalib-74ed4c** (*Diwan-E-Ghalib*): Paths as abasement, love's torment, or life's wandering ("lie down like a path"; "path of madness").[^14]

**unknown-angirasa-smrti-2bf9d1** (*Āṅgirasa Smṛti*): Ethical/scriptural course ("path of scripture").[^15]

**unknown-brhaspati-smrti-0fd070** (*Bṛhaspati Smṛti*): Moral deviation ("path of justice").[^16]

**unknown-kunjarakarna-dharmakathana-894f4a** (*Kuñjarakarṇa Dharmakathana*): Cosmic/spatial metaphor ("Crossroads of the Wind").[^17]

[^1]: Searches conducted for "road", "path", "highway", "\"the way\"", "\"the path\""; only works with at least one figurative instance listed.
